evidence.page_title: The Amazing Life of Sand | Deep Look | PBS LearningMedia
evidence.title: The Amazing Life of Sand | Deep Look | Grades 6-8, 9-12 Rock Cycle Teaching and Lesson Resources
evidence.description: There's a story in every grain of sand: tales of life and death, fire and water. If you scooped up a handful of sand from every beach, you'd have a history of the world sifting through your fingers. From mountain boulders to the shells of tiny ocean creatures, follow the journey that sand takes through thousands of years across entire continents to wind up stuck between your toes.

evidence.page_title: sand | Definition from the Geography topic
evidence.title: sand in Geography topic
evidence.description: sand in the Geography topic by Longman Dictionary of Contemporary English | LDOCE | What you need to know about Geography: words, phrases and expressions | Geography
evidence.text:
sand in Geography topic
From Longman Dictionary of Contemporary Englishsandsand1 /sænd/ ●●● S3 W3 noun
1 HEG[uncountable] a substance consisting of very small pieces of rocks and minerals, that forms beaches and deserts
a mixture of sand and cement
I have sand in my shoe.2 [countable, uncountable]SG an area of beach
miles of golden sands
We were just sitting on the sand.3 → the sands of timeTHESAURUS – Meanings 1 & 2sand a substance that forms beaches and deserts, or an area of this substancesunbathers lying on the sandHe could see several small figures out on the sands. beach an area of sand or small stones at the edge of the sea or a lakethe island's golden beachesHow about a day at the beach?a pebble beachdesert a large area of land where it is always very hot and dry, and there is a lot of sandthe Sahara desertThey crossed the desert by camel. dune/sand dune a hill made of sand near the sea or in the desertWe went for a long walk along the dunes. The dunes are home to a wide variety of birds and plants.Examples from the Corpussand• Everything about it was grey-brown - houses, donkeys, sand and peasants' heavy robes.• It is possible that the reactors will never be demolished, but will be buried in mounds of sand or gravel.• Just little puffs of sand in the short grass.• As they ran over the pulleys, a slurry of sand or tungsten carbide was poured on the wire.• The neighbors' children played happily in the sand.• You will notice phrases like crocodile tears, the elephant never forgets, and the ostrich burying its head in the sand.• Wet sand can be moulded, whilst dry sand is much more elusive.• All-terrain-vehicle tracks cross the white sand in the bed of the North Fork of the Red.• Here the effects of the weather - snow, wind, sand, rain and fog - are shown.

evidence.page_title: Heroic soldiers who fought Taliban attackers awarded Military Cross
evidence.title: Heroic soldiers who fought Taliban attackers awarded Military Cross
evidence.description: TWO brave soldiers who fought off Taliban attackers in separate skirmishes yesterday received the Military Cross at Buckingham palace.
evidence.text:
Heroic soldiers who fought Taliban attackers awarded Military Cross
TWO brave soldiers who fought off Taliban attackers in separate skirmishes yesterday received the Military Cross at Buckingham palace.
Marine Craig Buchanan, 26, survived a close-quarter insider attack by two Afghan policeman in October 2012 to kill one and drive the other off.
But by then two of his comrades were dead and only his swift first aid saved the life of a badly wounded comrade.
He held fire to ensure he did not shoot other members of his patrol in the dust and confusion before shooting the closest attacker.
His citation read: “His exemplary gallantry is an inspiration to others.”
And yesterday Mne Buchanan, from Zimbabwe, revealed that he and his comrades knew their attackers, having trained their attackers over about four months as part of the Coalition mentoring programme.
Mne Buchanan, who was with 40 Commando at the time, said: "We had shared food and stories with our attackers."
He was joined at the ceremony by his mother Barbara, 58, sister Michelle, 32, and brother Raymond, 34, who had flown in from South Africa.
He said: "It was a pretty hairy day. I went into autopilot. My training just kicked in.
"I was mega-surprised to get the medal. I thought the Colonel was going to give me a dressing down when he called me into his office.
"But this medal is testament to the other blokes around me on that day."
It was a pretty hairy day. I went into autopilot. My training just kicked in
Also receiving an MC from Prince Charles was Lance Corporal Tuljung Gurung, 28, of The Royal Gurkha Rifles who foiled a night-time insurgent attack in Afghanistan by fighting off his attacker with his bare hands and his huge kukri knife.
L/Cpl Gurung survived a grenade attack and being shot in the helmet when two men charged his post.
When one climbed into his 10ft watch tower, the brave Gurkha wrestled with him before slashing him with his kukri and driving both attackers off.
L/Cpl Gurung's wife Chandramaya, and his parents flew in from Nepal to attend the investiture and stood proudly when he was presented with the medal.
He said: "It's a great pleasure and honour to come over here and receive this great award from Prince Charles.
"My wife is very proud. This is a very precious time for us."

evidence.page_title: Burned bodies of rebels discovered in Syria | UK - Daily Express
evidence.title: WARNING - GRAPHIC CONTENT: Burned bodies of rebels discovered in Syria
evidence.description: SHOCKING new photos emerged today showing a pile of burned bodies believed to be Syrian rebels executed by government forces.
evidence.text:
WARNING - GRAPHIC CONTENT: Burned bodies of rebels discovered in Syria
SHOCKING new photos emerged today showing a pile of burned bodies believed to be Syrian rebels executed by government forces.
The bodies were found north of Damascus
The Syrian Arab News Agency took the photos near the city of Sidnaia, north of the capital Damascus.
The slaughter is the latest atrocity in the increasingly bloody conflict, in which an estimated 130,000 people have lost their lives, and nearly 10m people have been displaced.
The discovery came as Syrian Foreign Minister Walid Muallem threatened to walk out of peace talks in Geneva just hours after they began.
The regime and the opposition, who have refused to meet face to face, have been communicating through UN envoy Lakhdar Brahimi.
Mr Muallam has apparently told Mr Brahimi that he would abandon the discussions if "serious" talks had not begun by Saturday.
It is estimated that 130,000 people have not died in the conflict
The delegation, chosen by President Bashar Assad, met with the mediator for only 90 minutes today, after plans for direct talks between the two sides were scrapped.
Both sides blame each other for the descent into chaos.
"Transition to a free Syria is the key to fighting terror," said Oubai Shahbandar, a senior adviser to the Syrian opposition, which has demanded Assad's departure.
The conflict has destabilized the entire region and turned Syria into a magnet for al Qaeda-inspired militants.
Ayman al-Zawahr, the head of al Qaeda, yesterday called for rival Islamic groups to set aside a bitter dispute that "has bloodied our hearts" and instead focus on battling Assad's forces.
Infighting among rebels has left 1,400 people dead in the past 20 days, according to activists.
